questions.question: A document uses bold 14pt text for section titles instead of applying Heading 1 styles. How does this affect a screen reader user navigating the document?

options.text: The screen reader can still navigate by headings — visual formatting and semantic structure are the same
The screen reader cannot recognize these as headings and cannot navigate by heading list; the user must listen to the entire document
The screen reader announces the text twice — once as formatted, once as a heading
The document becomes completely inaccessible and cannot be read at all

questions.explanation: Screen readers detect semantic structure (heading styles), not visual formatting. Bold text is just bold text to a screen reader. When a screen reader user pulls up the 'Headings' list to navigate quickly, they get an empty list because the document has no heading styles applied. This is a common accessibility issue that the Accessibility Checker flags. The fix: select the bold text and apply Heading styles from the Styles pane.

questions.question: In PowerPoint, where should you check and adjust the reading order of elements on a slide?

options.text: In the View menu by switching to Outline view
In the Format menu by selecting the object and choosing 'Arrange'
In the Selection Pane (found in the Design or Accessibility menu) which shows all objects in reading order
Reading order is automatically correct in PowerPoint and cannot be adjusted

questions.explanation: The Selection Pane is the dedicated tool for managing reading order in PowerPoint. It displays all objects on a slide in the sequence they will be read by a screen reader. If the reading order doesn't match the visual layout, you can reorder objects using the up/down arrows in the Selection Pane. Using built-in slide layouts instead of custom text boxes typically ensures correct reading order by default.
